elements.sent-2411-001: Deep learning (DL) techniques have achieved significant success in various software engineering tasks (e.g., code completion by Copilot).
elements.sent-2411-002: However, DL systems are prone to bugs from many sources, including training data.
elements.sent-2411-003: Existing literature suggests that bugs in training data are highly prevalent, but little research has focused on understanding their impacts on the models used in software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we address this research gap through a comprehensive empirical investigation focused on three types of data prevalent in software engineering tasks: code-based, text-based, and metric-based.
elements.sent-2411-005: Using state-of-the-art baselines, we compare the models trained on clean datasets with those trained on datasets with quality issues and without proper preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-006: By analysing the gradients, weights, and biases from neural networks under training, we identify the symptoms of data quality and preprocessing issues.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our analysis reveals that quality issues in code data cause biased learning and gradient instability, whereas problems in text data lead to overfitting and poor generalisation of models.
elements.sent-2411-008: On the other hand, quality issues in metric data result in exploding gradients and model overfitting, and inadequate preprocessing exacerbates these effects across all three data types.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we demonstrate the validity and generalizability of our findings using six new datasets.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our research provides a better understanding of the impact and symptoms of data bugs in software engineering datasets.
elements.sent-2411-011: Practitioners and researchers can leverage these findings to develop better monitoring systems and data-cleaning methods to help detect and resolve data bugs in deep learning systems.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, deep learning models face unique challenges due to their complex architectures and data-driven paradigms~\cite{arpteg2018software .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Compared to traditional logic-driven software, DL-based software follows a data-driven computing paradigm where its models are trained using data.
elements.sent-2411-014: The training data can be noisy, biased, or incomplete, which may lead to unexpected or erroneous behaviours in the DL models~\cite{whang2020data .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Besides, deep learning models can be very complex and opaque, making it difficult to understand their decision process.
elements.sent-2411-016: Thus, the poor quality of training data in the DL models could pose significant challenges to their reliability and trustworthiness.
elements.sent-2411-017: DL bugs can originate from various sources, such as training data, hyperparameters, and model structure, and can lead to system crashes and unexpected runtime behaviour~\cite{islam2019fse .}
elements.sent-2411-018: They can also result in severe consequences, as evidenced by the fatal accidents involving self-driving cars~\cite{selfdrivingcarcrash, teslaAutopilotCrash .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The non-determinism of DL systems leads to different outcomes across multiple runs, making debugging challenging~\cite{nagarajan2018impact .}
elements.sent-2411-020: According to existing literature~\cite{islam2019fse , DL bugs can be divided into five categories: Data, Model, Structural, Non-Model Structural and API Bug.}
elements.sent-2411-021: They originate from errors in training data, such as incorrect labels, duplicates, out-of-distribution records, and missing values~\cite{islam2019fse, taxonomyRealFaults, cote2024data .}
elements.sent-2411-022: These errors can significantly affect the model performance~\cite{liang2022advances, sambasivan2021everyone .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Resolving data bugs is very challenging~\cite{yin2023dynamic, wang2024empirical .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Data bugs are hidden in the dataset and implicitly affect a model's behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-025: They also propagate to the model parameters during training, which makes their detection difficult.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, according to existing work~\cite{croft2022data , many existing benchmark datasets constructed by human annotators contain up to 70\% mislabeled data, which leads to data bugs in the DL models relying on those datasets~\cite{croft2022data .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: However, they primarily focus on the quality of the raw training data and overlook two crucial aspects: the preprocessing stage and the model training process.
elements.sent-2411-028: First, data preprocessing is essential for preparing the data in a suitable format for training.
elements.sent-2411-029: Errors, biases, or loss of information during preprocessing can propagate through the whole development steps of a model, significantly degrading the model's performance~\cite{amato2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Despite its importance, the impact of preprocessing on model performance has not been thoroughly investigated by the existing literature.
elements.sent-2411-031: Second, training is a crucial step in model development.
elements.sent-2411-032: Observing the training process and monitoring a model's internal state, such as gradients, weights, and biases, can offer valuable insights into how data quality issues affect the model's learning process.
elements.sent-2411-033: For example, if the model is experiencing vanishing or exploding gradients, they might indicate poor feature scaling or outliers in the data~\cite{glorot2010understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Similarly, analyzing the weights and biases of the model during training can indicate whether the model is learning meaningful patterns or is being misled by noisy or corrupted data points~\cite{koh2017understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Such insights can help practitioners take appropriate actions to mitigate the impact of data bugs in their DL-based applications.
elements.sent-2411-036: However, no existing work examines the impact of data bugs on the training behaviours of DL models.
elements.sent-2411-037: Our study aims to address this critical gap in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-038: First, we select three types of data for our analysis based on their frequent use in software engineering tasks: code-based, text-based, and metric-based.
elements.sent-2411-039: Second, we select state-of-the-art baseline models using these data types and compare their faulty models (containing data bugs) with corresponding bug-free versions.
elements.sent-2411-040: Third, we capture the detailed training logs using the Weights \& Biases framework and analyze the training metrics and model properties (e.g., gradients, weights, and biases) to derive meaningful insights.
elements.sent-2411-041: We perform qualitative and quantitative analyses of the gradients, weights, and biases to identify symptoms and manifestations of data quality and preprocessing issues.
elements.sent-2411-042: Finally, we validate our findings with new datasets to ensure the generalizability of our results.
elements.sent-2411-043: Thus, we answer four important research questions as follows: RQ1: How do data quality and preprocessing issues in code-based data affect the training behavior of deep learning models? We investigate the impact of data quality issues from two code-based datasets, Devign and BigVul~\cite{zhou2019devign, fan2020ac .}
elements.sent-2411-044: These datasets contain C/C++ functions from multiple projects and have known quality issues~\cite{croft2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Our analysis reveals that these issues cause gradient instability during training and significantly impair a model's capacity to capture complex patterns.
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, our manual analysis of attention weights shows that data quality issues in the code-based data can reduce the code comprehension abilities of the deep learning model.
elements.sent-2411-047: RQ2: How do data quality and preprocessing issues in text-based data affect the training behaviour of deep learning models? We assess the impact of data quality issues from two text-based datasets, Eclipse, maintained in Bugzilla and Hadoop, maintained in JIRA.
elements.sent-2411-048: Our study reveals that quality issues in text data lead to reduced abnormal weight distributions and overfitting of models to noisy patterns during model training.
elements.sent-2411-049: Our qualitative analysis using the t-SNE plots highlights the deep learning models' difficulty learning consistent feature representations from noisy data.
elements.sent-2411-050: RQ3: How do data quality and preprocessing issues in metric-based data affect the training behavior of deep learning models? We investigate the impact of data quality issues from two metric-based datasets, Openstack and QT, which frequently suffer from class imbalance problems~\cite{mcintosh2018fix, hoang2019deepjit .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our analysis reveals that quality issues in metric-based data lead to vanishing gradients and poor optimization during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, we perform qualitative analysis using GradCAM, an explainable AI technique for visual analysis of model inputs.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our qualitative analysis demonstrates that models trained on imbalanced data focus on irrelevant tokens and struggle to generalize to unseen data.
elements.sent-2411-054: RQ4: How well do our findings on data quality and preprocessing issues generalize to other code-based, text-based, and metric-based datasets? \looseness=-1 We evaluate the generalizability of our findings using six new datasets: D2A and Juliet (code-based), Spark and Mozilla (text-based), and Go and JDT (metric-based).
elements.sent-2411-055: Our analysis reveals that the data quality and processing issues in the training data lead to reduced model capacity, gradient instability, overfitting, and biased learning of the models, which align with our above findings.
elements.sent-2411-056: In contrast, the models trained on cleaned datasets show none of these issues.
elements.sent-2411-057: Such observations increase confidence in our findings and underscore the challenges of data bugs in deep learning models used in software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-058: Paper Organization: The remainder of the paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-059: Section 2 provides background knowledge about deep learning and explainable AI.
elements.sent-2411-060: Section 3 describes the methodology of our study in detail, including data type selection, data collection, experimental setup, post-hoc analysis and quantitative analysis.
elements.sent-2411-061: Section 4 talks about the findings for the different research questions.
elements.sent-2411-062: Section 5 discusses the implications of our findings for various stakeholders.
elements.sent-2411-064: Finally, Section 7 presents threats to the validity of our study, and Section 8 concludes the paper.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Data Quality Issues in Software Engineering Datasets
elements.sent-2411-065: Label Noise: Label noise refers to errors in the labels assigned to data instances in a dataset.
elements.sent-2411-066: These errors can stem from various sources, including insufficient information, annotator mistakes, subjective judgments, and data encoding issues~\cite{frenay2014comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-067: Label noise is prevalent in real-world datasets and thus can significantly impair the DL models using those datasets~\cite{frenay2013classification .}
elements.sent-2411-068: This issue is also relevant in software engineering datasets since they are often used to train DL models supporting code search, vulnerability detection, and program understanding~\cite{wang2024empirical, xu2024code, nie2023understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-069: For example, in a dataset for vulnerability detection, non-vulnerable code could be mistakenly labelled as vulnerable or vulnerable code could be labelled as non-vulnerable.
elements.sent-2411-070: Such label errors often result in inaccurate, biased, or flawed models~\cite{nie2023understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this work, we train multiple deep learning models using datasets containing label noise to determine its impact on the models.
elements.sent-2411-072: Class Imbalance: Class imbalance refers to a disproportionate representation of different classes within a dataset.
elements.sent-2411-073: It is also highly prevalent in various software engineering datasets, including API recommendation, code review automation, and defect prediction~\cite{irsan2023multi, tufano2022using, song2018comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-074: For example, in defect prediction, there are often significantly fewer defective modules than non-defective ones.
elements.sent-2411-075: Existing studies~\cite{mcintosh2018fix, kamei2012large, keshavarz2022apachejit, mahbub2023defectors show that only 5\%-26\% of the files contain the defective instances.}
elements.sent-2411-076: When trained on imbalanced datasets, DL models tend to be biased towards the majority class and demonstrate poor performance in identifying the minority class~\cite{giray2023use .}
elements.sent-2411-077: As software systems evolve, the characteristics of their data change, which leads to concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-078: This phenomenon is prevalent in many datasets, including the ones used for log-level prediction, anomaly detection and duplicate bug report detection~\cite{ma2018robust, ouatiti2024impact, zhang2023duplicate .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Recent research by Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2023duplicate revealed that most duplicate bug report detection techniques were only evaluated using data up to January 2014.}
elements.sent-2411-080: When these same techniques were applied to more recent data, their performance decreased significantly, highlighting the impact of data obsolescence on the effectiveness of deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-081: Attention-Based Analysis: Attention mechanisms help deep learning models focus on the most relevant parts of the input~\cite{vaswani2017attention .}
elements.sent-2411-082: Their inherent ability to assign importance weights to different input elements can also be leveraged to interpret the behaviours of the models.
elements.sent-2411-083: Recently, attention weights of input have been used to investigate the explainability of the deep learning models solving software engineering tasks~\cite{fu2022linevul, pornprasit2022deeplinedp, fu2022gpt2sp .}
elements.sent-2411-084: For example, Fu et al.~\cite{fu2022linevul leveraged the self-attention mechanism to explain the predictions of their proposed technique for vulnerability detection.}
elements.sent-2411-085: In this study, we employ their dataset and attention-based analysis to examine how data quality and preprocessing issues affect a DL model's training behaviour and learning capacity.
elements.sent-2411-086: These plots are often used to inspect how well a model has learned to differentiate among different classes in the data.
elements.sent-2411-087: By comparing t-SNE visualizations for different models and examining their separation of classes, we can evaluate their capacities to learn feature representations for a given task.
elements.sent-2411-088: This technique has also been used in several software engineering tasks, including duplicate bug report detection~\cite{messaoud2022duplicate .}
elements.sent-2411-089: As duplicate bug report detection is one of our selected tasks, we utilise t-SNE to explain and visualize the predictions of our trained model for this task.
elements.sent-2411-090: By analyzing the feature weights in the final convolutional layer of a convolutional neural network, Grad-CAM produces a heatmap highlighting the important regions in the input relevant to the model's output.
elements.sent-2411-091: In our study, we employ DeepJIT~\cite{hoang2019deepjit as a baseline technique for defect prediction.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Since DeepJIT is a CNN-based technique, we use Grad-CAM to understand and visualize the impact of input features on the model output.

elements.sent-2411-094: We discuss different steps of our study as follows.
elements.sent-2411-095: Selecting different types of data is crucial for our study since each data type has unique characteristics and quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-096: By examining multiple types of data, we can comprehensively investigate how data bugs manifest and affect deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-097: Based on the prevalence in software engineering datasets, Yang et al.~\cite{yang2022dlinse identified the three most prevalent types of data: code-based, text-based, and metric-based.}
elements.sent-2411-098: These data types have unique characteristics as follows.
elements.sent-2411-099: Code-based data: Code-based data is frequently used in training deep learning models that target various software engineering tasks, such as code clone detection, code generation, program repair, and vulnerability detection~\cite{fu2022linevul, zhang2020learning, li2017cclearner, tian2020evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-100: This data type encompasses source code files, test cases, and code changes~\cite{yang2022dlinse .}
elements.sent-2411-101: Text-based data: Natural language texts play a crucial role in numerous software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-102: Existing studies have used text-based data in deep learning techniques for software engineering tasks~\cite{yang2022dlinse .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Text-based data includes requirements specifications, design documents, code comments, commit messages, bug reports, user reviews, and question-answer posts from forums like Stack Overflow~\cite{he2020duplicate, ouatiti2024impact, mondal2024can .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Metric-based data: Metric-based data comprises various statistics derived by static analysis tools (e.g., SonarQube, Understand) from various software repositories.
elements.sent-2411-105: They quantify different aspects of source code, software design, development process, and software quality.
elements.sent-2411-106: Metric-based datasets have been used in several software engineering tasks such as defect prediction, effort estimation, code smell detection, and software maintainability assessment~\cite{hoang2019deepjit, liu2019deep, choetkiertikul2018deep .}
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, based on the prevalence of data types and their relevance to software engineering tasks, we consider code-based, text-based and metric-based data for our study (Step \circled{1 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-108: Selecting representative tasks for each type of data above is a critical step in our analysis (Step \circled{2 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-109: By carefully choosing the tasks that use our selected data types, we can examine how data bugs manifest and affect the training of deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-110: In particular, we have selected the following representative tasks for our analysis, as suggested by Yang et al.~\cite{yang2022dlinse : (a).}
elements.sent-2411-111: Vulnerability Detection: Detection of vulnerabilities in software code is one of the key applications where deep learning models show promising results.
elements.sent-2411-112: These models often use source code or binary for their detection task.. (b).
elements.sent-2411-113: Duplicate Bug Report Detection: Duplicate bug report detection is a prominent use case of text-based data from software engineering that is leveraged by DL models.
elements.sent-2411-114: The task's objective is to identify bug reports that describe the same underlying issue from previous bug reports.. (c).
elements.sent-2411-115: Defect Prediction: Predicting defects in code components using software metrics is a common application of deep learning in software engineering.
elements.sent-2411-116: This task involves predicting the defect proneness of software modules based on various code metrics (e.g., complexity, coupling, cohesion).
elements.sent-2411-117: After selecting the representative tasks, we choose multiple baseline approaches for the selected tasks.
elements.sent-2411-118: These approaches provide baseline deep learning models, which are used in our subsequent analysis.
elements.sent-2411-119: To select baselines for each of the types of data, we used the following criteria: (a) the technique should use a neural network model to perform one of the three tasks above, and (b) the technique should provide a comprehensive replication package with source code, datasets, and instructions for reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-120: Furthermore, we prioritized the baselines with clean and buggy versions in their replication packages.
elements.sent-2411-121: Based on these filtration criteria, we selected the following state-of-the-art baselines for our study.
elements.sent-2411-122: LineVul is a state-of-the-art transformer-based model for line-level vulnerability detection in code.
elements.sent-2411-123: The model takes code tokens as input and learns to predict the vulnerability status of each line.
elements.sent-2411-124: DCCNN, short for Duplicate bug report detection using Convolutional Neural Networks, employs a CNN-based architecture to identify duplicate bug reports from their textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-125: The CNN model learns to extract relevant features and patterns from a pair of bug reports and then predict whether they are duplicates or not.
elements.sent-2411-126: DeepJIT is an end-to-end deep learning framework for Just-In-Time (JIT) defect prediction.
elements.sent-2411-127: This technique computes software metrics from datasets of code changes and extracts salient features from commit messages.
elements.sent-2411-128: The learned features from commit messages and code changes are encoded into numerical matrices and then processed by separate CNN layers to predict whether the commit will likely introduce a defect.
elements.sent-2411-129: Besides the relevance to the selected data types, these baselines have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in their respective tasks, which justifies their selection for our study (Step \circled{3 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-130: We utilized six diverse datasets that were used by deep learning models targeting our tasks above: Devign and BigVul (code-based), Eclipse and Hadoop (text-based), and OpenStack and QT (metric-based).
elements.sent-2411-131: These datasets vary significantly in size, composition, and specific characteristics, allowing for comprehensive training and evaluation of deep learning models across different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-132: For each type of data, we chose two datasets to perform data source triangulation, per the guidelines by Runeson et al.~\cite{runeson2009guidelines , and to have multiple sources of evidence as recommended by Yin et al.~\cite{yin2009case .}}

elements.sent-2411-134: This section describes our experimental methodology, including how we configure our system, implement the baselines, and observe training behaviours.
elements.sent-2411-135: To reflect the original environments of our selected baselines, we use the following setup: (a) Code Editors: We use Visual Studio Code v1.79.0, which is a popular code editor for building DL-based applications~\cite{ide .}
elements.sent-2411-136: We also install the dependencies for each baseline into a separate virtual environment using the venv~\footnote{https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html module.}
elements.sent-2411-137: To prepare the baselines, we utilized the replication packages provided by the authors of the original studies~\cite{fu2022linevul, hoang2019deepjit, he2020duplicate .}
elements.sent-2411-138: This allowed us to accurately reproduce the baseline setups and maintain the integrity of their original implementations.
elements.sent-2411-139: We prepared three variants of the original baselines for our analysis, which are described below.. \looseness=-1 1.
elements.sent-2411-140: Baseline with Clean Data: First, we obtained clean datasets from existing studies~\cite{croft2023data, zhang2023duplicate, issta2021deepjit and prepared the original techniques to be trained using this high-quality data (Step \circled{4 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: This variant represents the ideal setup for the models as it is prepared with clean and high-quality data.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-142: Baseline with Buggy Data: To investigate the impact of data quality on model performance, we created a second variant prepared with data quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-143: We obtained buggy datasets from the same existing studies~\cite{croft2023data, zhang2023duplicate, issta2021deepjit and configured the baseline models to be trained using this buggy data (Step \circled{5 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-144: This step will allow us to observe how bugs in the training data might affect the model's learning process and subsequent performance.. \looseness=-1 3.
elements.sent-2411-145: Baseline with Missing Preprocessing: For our third variant, we focused on preprocessing faults, which are among the most prevalent types of faults in deep learning, according to the existing taxonomy~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults .}
elements.sent-2411-146: We removed the preprocessing operations from the training pipeline in preparation for training (Step \circled{6 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-147: By setting up the model to simulate the missing preprocessing operation, we aim to understand how the absence of these crucial steps might impact a model's performance and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-148: To better understand the training process and model behaviour, we integrated Weights and Biases (W\&B) logging~\cite{wandb into the existing baselines and their variants (Step \circled{7 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: After successfully integrating W\&B, we trained the models and monitored and captured their higher-level training, such as the model's loss and accuracy during the training.
elements.sent-2411-150: Additionally, we logged lower-level statistics such as gradients, weights, and biases from all model layers.
elements.sent-2411-151: This allowed us to track a model's progress and performance over time.
elements.sent-2411-152: Furthermore, we logged the runtime configurations to capture the computational environment in which the experiments were conducted.
elements.sent-2411-153: Finally, to account for the variability and stochasticity inherent in neural networks, we ran each baseline model and corresponding variants fifteen times~\cite{arcuri2014hitchhiker .}
elements.sent-2411-154: We use the gradients, weights, and biases over multiple runs and calculate aggregated statistics for each layer of the models (Step \circled{7 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Table.~\ref{tab:weight_metrics discusses the aggregate metrics used for analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-156: By examining these aggregated statistics, we identified symptoms and manifestations of data quality and preprocessing issues and quantified their impact on deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-157: We analyzed the statistical differences between models trained on clean datasets and those trained on buggy datasets to identify if there were any differences in their performance.
elements.sent-2411-158: We observed how data bugs and preprocessing issues affected different layers' weights, biases, and gradients.
elements.sent-2411-159: By quantifying the impact on specific parts of the models, such as attention layers, convolutional layers, and fully connected layers, we gained insights into how data quality and preprocessing issues can degrade the performance and reliability of deep learning models in software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-160: To gain deeper insights into the impact of data bugs on our deep learning models, we conducted a post-hoc analysis using explainable AI techniques (Step \circled{8 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-161: These techniques help us understand how the models make predictions and identify the influential parts of the input data.
elements.sent-2411-162: For the code-based data, we analyzed LineVul's attention weights.
elements.sent-2411-163: Since the LineVul model is attention-based, an analysis of its attention weights helps us identify the parts of the input code that the model focuses on when making predictions about vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-164: By comparing the attention weights from models trained on clean and buggy data, we observed how data bugs affect a model's attention and potentially lead to incorrect predictions.
elements.sent-2411-165: For the text-based data, we utilized t-SNE (t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding) visualization~\cite{van2008visualizing .}
elements.sent-2411-166: By applying t-SNE to the representations learned by the DCCNN model at different layers, we visualized the decision boundary for duplicate and non-duplicate bug reports.
elements.sent-2411-167: This helps us understand how the learned representations differ between models trained on clean and buggy data, providing insights into the impact of data bugs on the model's learning process.
elements.sent-2411-168: For the metric-based data, we implemented GradCAM~\cite{selvaraju2017grad .}
elements.sent-2411-169: In the context of defect prediction using DeepJIT, GradCAM helps us visualize which metrics and parts of the input features are most influential in the model's decision.
elements.sent-2411-170: By contrasting the GradCAM outputs of models trained on clean data against those trained on buggy data, we identify how data bugs affect the model's focus and potentially lead to incorrect defect predictions.

elements.sent-2411-179: In our study, we examined the effects of four common data bugs: label noise, class imbalance, concept drift, and missing preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-180: To address each research question, we constructed and trained a comprehensive dataset of 120 buggy models, with 30 models dedicated to each of the four bug types.

elements.sent-2411-183: This section presents our findings, focusing on the most common symptoms of these data bugs across three data types: code-based, text-based, and metric-based.

elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Impact analysis of Label Noise using Attention Mechanisms
elements.sent-2411-218: In our study, we also analyze the attention weights of a model to understand the impact of data quality issues on the model's training.
elements.sent-2411-219: Our study examined various data quality issues, and we found that label noise had the strongest impact on models trained with code-based data (see Table~\ref{tab:rq1Table ).}
elements.sent-2411-220: Based on this finding, we decided to conduct a qualitative analysis to gain a deeper understanding of how label noise affects code-based data.
elements.sent-2411-221: To investigate this issue, we trained 60 models in total.
elements.sent-2411-222: We divided these models into four groups of 15 models each: (a) models trained on a clean Devign dataset, (b) models trained on a clean BigVul dataset, (c) models trained on a Devign dataset with label noise, and (d) models trained on a BigVul dataset with label noise.
elements.sent-2411-223: For each group, we calculated the average attention weights across all 15 models.
elements.sent-2411-224: This approach helps us identify consistent patterns and mitigate the impact of non-determinism in deep learning.
elements.sent-2411-226: Bug-free Data: Models trained on bug-free datasets on vulnerability detection demonstrate a strong focus on tokens critical for identifying potential security flaws.
elements.sent-2411-227: This focus is evident in the BigVul dataset, which contains source code from Chrome, Linux, Android, and Tcpdump.
elements.sent-2411-228: When analyzing models trained on this dataset, we observe high attention weights on key functions and data structures related to security vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-229: For instance, rds6\_inc\_info\_copy (87.3796) and struct rds\_incoming (64.1523) receive significant attention, indicating their relevance to network-related vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-230: The models also heavily emphasize network fields such as laddr (85.7431) and faddr (86.2221), demonstrating their awareness of potential security issues in network communications.
elements.sent-2411-231: The Devign dataset contains source code from Linux, FFmpeg, Qemu, and Wireshark.
elements.sent-2411-232: Models trained on this dataset focus on memory-related operations and type conversions, which are one of the primary causes of security vulnerabilities~\cite{Stevens_Fenner_Rudoff_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-233: Tokens like uint16\_t (88.8645) and uint64\_t (86.9365) receive very high attention weights, which reflects their importance to proper data handling.
elements.sent-2411-234: Critical function calls for cross-platform compatibility, such as le16\_to\_cpu (82.2068) and le64\_to\_cpu (80.8885), are also strongly emphasized.
elements.sent-2411-235: Models trained on both datasets show significant attention to error-checking patterns and memory operations like sizeof (30.5034) and if(!conn) (32.1079).
elements.sent-2411-236: These attention weights suggest the models have developed a comprehensive understanding of code elements related to security flaws from the datasets.
elements.sent-2411-237: Buggy Data: In contrast, the models trained on buggy data showed their inability to focus on tokens crucial for vulnerability detection.
elements.sent-2411-238: In BigVul, the model assigns significant weights to non-vulnerable tokens or variables such as int (37.6882), rt (36.5832), and nl (41.7406).
elements.sent-2411-239: Similarly, in Devign, the model focuses its attention on various elements like static (50.7911), CCIDBus (55.7003), and Device (43.4922).
elements.sent-2411-240: In other words, the model fails to prioritize the tokens that might signal potential vulnerabilities, such as unsafe operations or improper error handling.
elements.sent-2411-241: Moreover, the models trained on data with label noise pay considerable attention to non-informative tokens, such as newline characters (26.1465 in BigVul, 54.1878 in Devign) and empty tokens (8.2194 in BigVul, 10.1193 in Devign).
elements.sent-2411-242: This diffused attention pattern, evident across both datasets, suggests that these models struggle to distinguish between benign code patterns and those that may introduce vulnerabilities.
elements.sent-2411-243: This limits their effectiveness in identifying security-critical code sections.
elements.sent-2411-244: Furthermore, missing preprocessing can lead to slow convergence, incorrect learning patterns, and distorted distributions, impairing the models' ability to understand and process code effectively.. \end{rqbox.}
elements.subsection-2411-010: RQ2: How do data quality and preprocessing issues in text-based data affect the training behaviour of deep learning models?
elements.sent-2411-245: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:rq2Table , 93.33\% of the models trained on data with concept drift exhibited this phenomenon.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Our analysis of these models revealed that 80-85\% of their layers were affected, particularly the convolutional and dense layers.
elements.sent-2411-247: These layers demonstrated unusually high weight variances (e.g., $\sigma^2 \approx 2.3$), significantly deviating from their baseline counterparts (e.g., mean weight variance $\sigma^2 \approx 0.64$).
elements.sent-2411-248: The observed abnormal weight distribution can be attributed to the dynamic nature of text data in software engineering.
elements.sent-2411-249: As software and technology evolve, the corresponding text-based data also changes, which causes shifts in the underlying data distribution.
elements.sent-2411-250: This evolution in software and text can affect how the text is represented, and the relevant weights and biases are learned by the models.
elements.sent-2411-251: As a consequence, older and newer data points might require different sets of optimal parameters.
elements.sent-2411-252: During model training, the text data with concept drift generate large gradient signals to push the parameter values towards the optimal settings for their respective time periods, which could have led to abnormal weight distribution in the models.
elements.sent-2411-253: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:rq2Table , 76.67\% of the models trained on data with concept drift and 56.67\% with label noise exhibited this phenomenon.}
elements.sent-2411-254: Our analysis revealed that 80-90\% of the layers in these models were affected, particularly the convolutional layers and batch normalization layers.
elements.sent-2411-255: The gradient distribution of these layers demonstrated substantial skewness ($\gamma \approx 2.9$ for models trained with concept drift, and $\gamma \approx 1.2$ for models trained with label noise), far exceeding the baseline skewness values ($\gamma \approx 0.2$).
elements.sent-2411-256: As discussed above, concept drift in text-based data can lead to large gradient signals during training.
elements.sent-2411-257: This not only affects weight distribution but also could make the gradients skewed.
elements.sent-2411-258: Large gradient signals shift the overall distribution towards higher values, causing positive skewness.
elements.sent-2411-259: Similarly, label noise can also cause gradient skewness.
elements.sent-2411-260: In our datasets with label noise, similar data points have different labels due to incorrect labelling.
elements.sent-2411-261: When a model encounters these mislabeled examples during training, it tries to learn a decision boundary that separates these similar points with different labels, which is theoretically impossible.
elements.sent-2411-262: This leads to large prediction errors, as the model's output significantly differs from the (incorrectly assigned) target labels.
elements.sent-2411-263: These large errors, in turn, result in large gradient updates during backpropagation.
elements.sent-2411-264: As the training process continues, these inconsistent and large gradient updates accumulate, causing the overall gradient distribution to be skewed.
elements.sent-2411-265: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:rq2Table , 86.66\% of models trained on data with class imbalance exhibited overfitting.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Our analysis revealed that 60-65\% of the layers in these models were affected, particularly the dense layers.
elements.sent-2411-267: The median bias value of these layers ($med \approx 1.1$) is much higher than their baseline counterparts ($med \approx 0.07$).
elements.sent-2411-268: When a dataset has a disproportionate number of samples in one class compared to the others, the model tends to become biased towards the majority class.
elements.sent-2411-269: As a result, the model's biases become skewed towards the majority class, which results in the model overfitting the training data.

elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Qualitative Analysis: Impact of Concept Drift on Decision Boundary
elements.sent-2411-287: To assess the impact of data quality issues on deep learning models' training behaviour, we also performed a qualitative analysis using t-SNE plots.
elements.sent-2411-288: First, we trained 30 models on clean datasets and 30 models on datasets containing concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-289: We selected concept drift for our qualitative analysis, as it affected the highest number of models (see Table.~\ref{tab:rq2Table ).}
elements.sent-2411-290: Then, we generated the t-SNE plots for the dense layer representations, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:bugfreeDense and Fig.~\ref{fig:buggyDense .}}
elements.sent-2411-291: We focus on dense layers since their decision boundaries capture complex, non-linear relationships between features and directly contribute to the final classification.
elements.sent-2411-292: Furthermore, existing literature~\cite{messaoud2022duplicate has analyzed the t-SNE plots of dense layers in their qualitative analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-293: We discuss the outcome of our analysis below.
elements.sent-2411-294: From Fig.~\ref{fig:bugfreeDense and~\ref{fig:buggyDense , we note the impact of concept drift on the dense layers of our analyzed deep learning models for duplicate bug report detection.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: In the first dense layer (Fig.~\ref{fig:bugfreeDense (a)), models trained on clean data show a clear separation between the two classes, indicating effective capture of the patterns.}
elements.sent-2411-296: On the other hand, models trained on buggy data (Fig.~\ref{fig:buggyDense (a)) exhibit complex representations with ambiguous decision boundaries.}
elements.sent-2411-297: This observation is supported by an analysis of KL Divergence, which serves as the cost function of the t-SNE plot.
elements.sent-2411-298: As the cost function, KL Divergence is automatically calculated and optimized during the generation of these visualizations, with lower KL Divergence values indicating a better representation of the high-dimensional data in the 2D space.
elements.sent-2411-299: For the first dense layer, the mean KL divergence for buggy models (0.32) is higher than that of bug-free models (0.26), indicating more noise in the layer representation of models affected by concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-300: The second dense layer (Fig.\ref{fig:bugfreeDense (b) and Fig.\ref{fig:buggyDense (b)) demonstrates similar trends, with bug-free models showing well-defined decision boundaries and buggy data models displaying unclear decision boundaries and fragmented clusters.}}
elements.sent-2411-301: This trend is further corroborated by the KL divergence analysis, where buggy models exhibit a mean KL divergence of 0.24 compared to 0.14 for bug-free models in the second dense layer.
elements.sent-2411-302: The increase in KL divergence for buggy models across both layers (23\% in the first layer and 71\% in the second layer) underscores the concept drift's impact on the network.
elements.sent-2411-303: It also highlights how concept drift progressively affects a model's ability to learn meaningful representations and make accurate predictions, ultimately impairing its performance in duplicate bug report detection.

elements.sent-2411-307: In these layers, up to 85\% of the neurons showed no change in their gradients.
elements.sent-2411-308: That is, 85\% of the neurons in a particular layer have not received any updates and have effectively stopped learning.
elements.sent-2411-309: This phenomenon can be explained by the disproportionate representation of classes in imbalanced datasets.
elements.sent-2411-310: In such datasets, the model is exposed to significantly more examples from the majority classes, causing neurons associated with these classes to receive frequent and substantial gradient updates.
elements.sent-2411-311: Conversely, neurons detecting minority classes receive updates far less frequently and with smaller magnitudes.
elements.sent-2411-312: As a consequence, a significant portion of neurons, particularly those responsible for minority class features, become `dead' and do not contribute to the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-313: Thus, the model struggles to learn representations for minority classes, leading to poor performance in these underrepresented categories.
elements.sent-2411-314: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:rq3Table , 83.33\% of the models trained on imbalanced datasets exhibited this phenomenon.}
elements.sent-2411-315: In these models, gradient magnitudes decreased exponentially from the output layer towards the input layer.
elements.sent-2411-316: Similarly, models trained on corrupted data experienced gradient drops by a factor of $10^6$ between the output and input layers.
elements.sent-2411-317: In contrast, models trained on clean data showed a moderate drop in gradients, with only a $10^2$ factor decrease from output to input.
elements.sent-2411-318: As discussed above, class imbalance leads to sparse parameter updates, which in turn contributes to the vanishing gradient problem.
elements.sent-2411-319: As neurons associated with minority classes receive infrequent and smaller updates, their gradient signals become progressively weaker as they propagate backwards from the output layer through the network.
elements.sent-2411-320: Consequently, the early layers of the network (e.g., input layers), crucial for learning fundamental features, receive negligible updates for minority class patterns and suffer from vanishing gradients.
elements.sent-2411-321: This phenomenon severely impairs a model's ability to learn from minority classes, further exacerbating the issues caused by class imbalance.

elements.sent-2411-335: These extreme gradient values can be explained by the lack of appropriate feature scaling and normalization~\cite{glorot2010understanding .}
elements.sent-2411-336: Without these preprocessing steps, the model encounters features with vastly different scales, leading to disproportionate weight updates.
elements.sent-2411-337: This leads to high volatility and significant variance in the gradient magnitudes, which results in exploding gradients and potential divergence from the normal behaviours in the neural networks~\cite{pascanu2013difficulty, goodfellow2016deep .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Our analysis revealed that 53.33\% of the models exhibited this issue, with 85-90\% of their layers affected, particularly the input and early hidden layers.
elements.sent-2411-339: These layers demonstrated high variance in weight magnitudes ($\sigma^2 \approx 5.76$), which is significantly higher than their baseline counterparts ($\sigma^2 \approx 0.89$) collected from the models trained on normalized data.
elements.sent-2411-340: This high variance in weight distribution stems from the interaction within a neural network and the diverse scales inherent in software metrics.
elements.sent-2411-341: For example, lines of code might be in thousands, while cyclomatic complexity might be in single digits.
elements.sent-2411-342: During training, the neural network compensates for these scale disparities to ensure a fair representation of all features in the learning process~\cite{lecun2002efficient .}
elements.sent-2411-343: It assigns larger weights to small-scale features (e.g., cyclomatic complexity) to amplify their effect and smaller weights to large-scale features (e.g., lines of code) to prevent them from dominating the output.
elements.sent-2411-344: This compensation mechanism results in a wide range of weight values.
elements.sent-2411-345: The input and early hidden layers are particularly susceptible to this issue as they directly interact with the raw feature values~\cite{lecun2002efficient , contributing to the observed weight variance issue.}
elements.subsubsection-2411-012: Qualitative Analysis: Impact of Class Imbalance on Feature Importance
elements.sent-2411-346: To analyze the impact of class imbalance on the buggy and bug-free models, we apply the GradCAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping) technique~\cite{selvaraju2017grad .}
elements.sent-2411-347: This approach has been used in previous studies to understand the effectiveness and efficacy of defect prediction models~\cite{issta2021deepjit .}
elements.sent-2411-348: For our GradCAM analysis, we used datasets specifically designed for defect prediction, focusing on software metrics.
elements.sent-2411-349: Our dataset exhibited an imbalanced distribution, with the minor class comprising non-defective instances and the major class consisting of defective instances.
elements.sent-2411-350: By examining the GradCAM outputs, we investigate which metrics the models prioritize and how this focus differs between the two types of models trained on two distinct codebases: Openstack and QT.
elements.sent-2411-351: Buggy Models: Our analysis of the GradCAM output for the buggy models reveals a disproportionate focus on obscure or overly specific tokens across both codebases.
elements.sent-2411-352: In the OpenStack model, tokens like `audit\_location\_generator' (0.9987) and `i62ce43a330d7ae94eda4' (0.9962) have probabilities very close to 1, while more general programming concepts like `if' (0.0023) and `return' (0.0041) have probabilities near 0.

elements.subsection-2411-012: RQ4: How well do our findings on data quality and preprocessing issues generalize to other code-based, text-based, and metric-based datasets?
elements.sent-2411-367: To evaluate the generalizability of our findings, we conducted experiments on six additional datasets: D2A and Juliet (code-based), Spark and Mozilla (text-based), and Go and JDT (metric-based).
elements.sent-2411-369: In code-based datasets, we found that 83.33\% of models (25 out of 30) exhibited inconsistent learning, 86.67\% (26 out of 30) showed reduced model capacity, and 76.67\% (23 out of 30) experienced gradient instability when trained on data with label noise or concept drift.
elements.sent-2411-370: For text-based datasets, 93.33\% of models (28 out of 30) showed abnormal weight distribution, 76.67\% (23 out of 30) demonstrated gradient skewness, and 86.67\% (26 out of 30) exhibited overfitting when trained on data with concept drift or class imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-371: In metric-based datasets, 76.67\% of models (23 out of 30) showed sparse parameter updates, 83.33\% (26 out of 30) experienced vanishing gradients, and 73.33\% (22 out of 30) demonstrated poor optimization when trained on data with label noise or class imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-372: When models were trained without appropriate preprocessing of data, we also observed similar patterns across all dataset types.

elements.sent-2411-393: To assess the generalizability of our findings, we performed statistical significance tests with the following hypotheses. \begin{equation}H_0 : The presence of data quality issues and model \phantom{:} symptoms are independent H_1 : The presence of data quality issues and model \phantom{:} symptoms are dependent\end{equation} We selected McNemar’s test~\cite{pembury2020effective for the statistical tests due to our experimental design and paired nominal data, as it specifically evaluates changes in binary outcomes (presence/absence of symptoms) within the same models.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}H_0 : The presence of data quality issues and model \phantom{:} symptoms are independent H_1 : The presence of data quality issues and model \phantom{:} symptoms are dependent\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-394: While McNemar's test establishes statistical significance to test our null hypothesis that data quality issues and model symptoms are independent, the odds ratio measures the strength of their dependence~\cite{szumilas2010explaining .}
elements.sent-2411-395: In our analysis, an odds ratio of 1 would support our null hypothesis, which indicates that there is no association between data quality issues and model symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-396: Conversely, odds ratios greater than 1 would support our alternative hypothesis, suggesting that the presence of data quality issues increases the likelihood of observed symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-397: Moreover, odds ratios below 1 would indicate that data quality issues decrease the likelihood of model symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-398: McNemar's test results consistently rejected the null hypothesis across all symptoms ($p < 0.01$).
elements.sent-2411-399: The odds ratios, measuring the strength of association between data quality issues and symptoms, were substantially greater than 1 in all cases, ranging from $15.53$ for abnormal weight distribution to $806.10$ for gradient skewness, strongly supporting our alternative hypothesis.
elements.sent-2411-400: The most pronounced dependencies were observed in inconsistent learning, gradient instability, and overfitting ($p < 0.0001$), indicating that these symptoms were exclusively present when data quality issues existed.
elements.sent-2411-401: Even the weakest association, found in the symptom "high variance in weight distribution" ($p = 0.0098$), demonstrated a statistically significant association between the symptom and data quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-402: These results demonstrate that addressing data quality issues and applying appropriate proper preprocessing operations to training data can significantly improve model training for various software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-403: The consistent improvements observed across different dataset types further validate the generalizability of our findings and underscore the critical importance of data quality and preprocessing in machine learning applications for software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-404: The repeated emergence of similar patterns and enhancements after addressing the data quality issues across multiple datasets increases confidence in our study findings.. \end{rqbox.}
elements.sent-2411-405: The findings of our study have significant implications for various stakeholders involved in developing and deploying deep learning models targeting software engineering tasks.
elements.sent-2411-406: We discuss these implications in detail below.
elements.sent-2411-407: Researchers: Researchers can reuse our dataset and leverage our empirical insights concerning label noise, concept drift, and class imbalance to develop robust data cleaning and preprocessing techniques.
elements.sent-2411-408: Our findings on the impact of missing preprocessing operations can also guide researchers in designing appropriate preprocessing pipelines tailored to different data types (code-based, text-based, and metric-based).
elements.sent-2411-409: Additionally, our analysis of how data quality issues affect the training of DL models can inform the development of more effective debugging techniques for DL models.
elements.sent-2411-410: Furthermore, researchers can integrate the explainable AI (XAI) techniques demonstrated in our study, such as attention visualization, t-SNE, and GradCAM, into their methodologies to better understand model behaviour and the impact of data quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-411: Finally, the researchers can use the symptoms of data bugs as verification criteria to enhance the reproducibility of deep learning bugs, as highlighted by existing literature~\cite{shah2025towards .}
elements.sent-2411-412: Software Engineers: Software engineers can leverage our insights and implement continuous data monitoring systems that track properties such as weight distributions, gradient patterns, and learning stability across different layers.
elements.sent-2411-413: These systems can be used to automatically detect anomalies in training patterns and data distributions, enabling early identification of potential data quality issues before they are deployed in real-world systems.
elements.sent-2411-414: Our insights on the effects of concept drift can be used to establish processes for regular model retraining and deployment, ensuring their relevance over time.
elements.sent-2411-415: Moreover, the XAI techniques from our study can help software engineers debug and analyze model behaviour, identify potential biases, and address any issues before model deployment.
elements.sent-2411-416: Deep Learning Engineers: Deep learning engineers can leverage our empirical findings to establish automated data quality assurance, design validation mechanisms and integrate them into MLOps pipelines.
elements.sent-2411-417: The identified data quality issues and their manifestations can serve as the basis for designing effective validation rules, statistical tests, and domain-specific constraints.
elements.sent-2411-418: Furthermore, our study emphasizes the importance of robust preprocessing pipelines, which can guide deep learning engineers in building modular and extensible preprocessing components that adapt to different data types and evolving data characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-419: Our findings enable deep learning engineers to develop optimized training procedures with built-in validation mechanisms to ensure stable model convergence.
elements.sent-2411-420: Additionally, they can implement comprehensive monitoring and alerting systems that track critical indicators like weight distributions, gradient flows, and learning patterns, allowing rapid detection and response to potential data quality issues during model training.

elements.sent-2411-434: In summary, the existing studies have examined the impact of data quality on model performance.

elements.sent-2411-466: In this study, we investigate the impact of data quality and preprocessing issues on the training behaviours of deep learning models targeting software engineering tasks.

elements.sent-2411-482: A data bug is a systematic error or flaw in the data pipeline that leads to incorrect, inconsistent, or misleading data, thereby compromising the integrity or performance of a machine learning or data-driven system~\cite{islam2019fse .}
elements.sent-2411-483: Formally, for a data pipeline \( \mathcal{P \) that transforms raw data \( X_{\text{raw \) into processed data \( X_{\text{proc \), a data bug exists if: \begin{equation}\exists\ E \in P\ such that\ E(X_{raw}) \neq X_{expected},\end{equation} where \( X_{\text{expected \) is the correct output of the pipeline under ideal conditions.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\exists\ E \in P\ such that\ E(X_{raw}) \neq X_{expected},\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-484: Data bugs can arise from issues such as data collection from unreliable sources, faulty transformations, incorrect feature engineering, erroneous dimensionality reduction, and inappropriate data type conversions that alter the fundamental nature of the data.
elements.sent-2411-485: They may also arise from missing preprocessing steps, such as failure to handle missing values, overlooking duplicate entries, and neglecting the inconsistencies in the data.
elements.sent-2411-486: Class Imbalance: Class imbalance refers to a scenario where the distribution of classes in the dataset is highly skewed.
elements.sent-2411-487: Formally, given a dataset \( \mathcal{D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1 ^N \) with \( y_i \in \{1, 2, ..., K\} \), class imbalance exists if the class prior probabilities \( P(Y=k) \) satisfy: \begin{equation}\exists\ k, l \in \{1, ..., K\}\ such that\ \frac{P(Y=k)}{P(Y=l)} \geq \tau,\end{equation} where \( \tau \gg 1 \) is a threshold indicating significant disparity (e.g., \( \tau = 10 \) for severe imbalance).}}
elements.sent-2411-488: For instance, in software engineering domains, moderate imbalance is characterized by \( \tau \approx 1.5 \) (corresponding to ratios like 60:40) and might be acceptable.
elements.sent-2411-489: However, severe imbalance occurs at \( \tau \geq 3 \) (corresponding to ratios of 75:25 or greater), which is undesirable~\cite{mahbub2023defectors,li2022robust .}
elements.sent-2411-490: Concept Drift: Concept drift refers to a change in the underlying data distribution over time, specifically in the relationship between input features \( X \) and the target variable \( Y \).
elements.sent-2411-491: For a time-varying distribution \( P_t(X, Y) \), concept drift occurs between times \( t_1 \) and \( t_2 \) if: \begin{equation}P_{t_1}(Y \mid X) \neq P_{t_2}(Y \mid X),\end{equation} even if \( P_{t_1 (X) = P_{t_2 (X) \).}}
elements.sent-2411-492: This distinguishes it from covariate shift (where only \( P(X) \) changes).
elements.sent-2411-493: Label Noise: Label noise arises when training labels are corrupted.
elements.sent-2411-494: For a true labeling function \( f: \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{Y \), observed labels \( \tilde{y _i \) are perturbed such that: \begin{equation}\tilde{y}_i =  y_i with probability  1-\eta, z \sim Y \setminus \{y_i\} with probability  \eta, \end{equation} where \( \eta \in [0, 1] \) is the noise rate~\cite{frenay2014comprehensive .}}}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\tilde{y}_i =  y_i with probability  1-\eta, z \sim Y \setminus \{y_i\} with probability  \eta, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-495: Label noise can be uniform (random flips) or structured (biased toward specific classes).
elements.sent-2411-496: Missing Preprocessing Operations: Missing preprocessing operations occur when input data deviates from the expected distribution assumed by a deep learning (DL) model due to omitted transformations.
elements.sent-2411-497: Let \( T: \mathcal{X \rightarrow \mathcal{X ' \) denote a required preprocessing function (e.g., normalization, imputation).}}
elements.sent-2411-498: If raw data \( X \) is fed to a model trained on \( T(X) \), the input distribution mismatch degrades the model's performance.
elements.sent-2411-499: Formally, the model \( M \) optimized for \( P(T(X)) \) faces a distribution shift when applied to \( P(X) \), violating the i.i.d.. assumption.
elements.sent-2411-500: In other words, the missing preprocessing operations introduce a shift in the distribution between the data the model was trained on and the data it encounters during inference.
elements.sent-2411-501: This shift in the distribution violates the assumption that the distributions during training and inference will be identical, and this causes a drop in the model's performance.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Preprocessing Faults in Deep Learning Systems
elements.sent-2411-502: In this section, we discuss the most common types of preprocessing faults in deep learning systems, as per the existing taxonomy by Humbatova et al.~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults .}
elements.sent-2411-503: These faults broadly manifest in two categories: missing preprocessing and incorrect preprocessing.
elements.sent-2411-504: Missing Preprocessing: Missing preprocessing refers to a scenario when a preprocessing step leading to better performance has not been applied at all.
elements.sent-2411-505: As suggested by the existing literature~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults , missing preprocessing can significantly impact the model performance.}
elements.sent-2411-506: For code analysis, cases were reported where the lexer and parser failed to catch certain code structures due to missing preprocessing steps.
elements.sent-2411-507: In text processing, particularly with social media data and software engineering text, issues arose from not handling special characters and different encodings (UTF-8, Unicode).
elements.sent-2411-508: With metric-based data, the absence of correlation analysis between features led to redundant features in the training data, and missing normalization for numerical values affected model performance.
elements.sent-2411-509: Incorrect Preprocessing: Incorrect preprocessing refers to a scenario when a preprocessing step was either inappropriate or incorrect.
elements.sent-2411-510: According to existing literature~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults , incorrect preprocessing can manifest in subtle ways.}
elements.sent-2411-511: In code preprocessing, incorrect token replacement and lexer implementations led to improper parsing of programming constructs.
elements.sent-2411-512: For text data, developers encountered problems with incorrect handling of special tokens, where preprocessing tokens were incompatible with the existing toolchain.
elements.sent-2411-513: One specific case involved the Stanford Parser producing incorrect tags for certain words, leading to the wrong interpretation of the data.
elements.sent-2411-514: For metric-based features, when different features of the numerical data were scaled inconsistently across different ranges (e.g., [0,1] versus [-1,1]), this resulted in performance degradation~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults .}
elements.sent-2411-515: In this study, we focus exclusively on missing preprocessing faults for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-516: First, missing preprocessing faults occur more frequently in deep learning systems than incorrect preprocessing~\cite{taxonomyRealFaults .}
elements.sent-2411-517: Second, missing preprocessing can be systematically studied by removing the preprocessing steps.
elements.sent-2411-518: However, there currently exists no systematic approach for simulating wrong preprocessing, as existing mutation techniques focus primarily on training, model, and data faults~\cite{humbatova2021deepcrime .}
elements.sent-2411-519: Third, manually selecting the incorrect preprocessing operations could introduce bias, leading to unreliable findings.
elements.sent-2411-520: Selecting representative tasks for each type of data above is a critical step for our analysis (Step \circled{2 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-521: In particular, we have selected classification tasks for our analysis due to their several benefits.
elements.sent-2411-522: First, classification-based tasks tackle many important challenges in software engineering (e.g., vulnerability detection, bug localization, duplicate bug report detection, code clone detection), leveraging different types of data.
elements.sent-2411-523: On the other hand, generation tasks in software engineering are primarily focused on code~\cite{yang2022dlinse .}
elements.sent-2411-524: Thus, selecting generation tasks could limit our ability to study the impact of data quality and preprocessing issues across different types of data.
elements.sent-2411-525: Second, classification tasks provide straightforward evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, precision, recall) for measuring the impact of data quality issues, which is crucial for our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-526: Thus, we have selected the following representative tasks for our analysis, as suggested by Yang et al.~\cite{yang2022dlinse : (a) Vulnerability Detection: Detection of vulnerabilities in software code is one of the key applications where deep learning models show promising results.}
elements.sent-2411-527: These models often use source code or binary for their detection task.
elements.sent-2411-528: The task's objective is to identify the incoming bug reports that describe the same underlying issue from the past bug reports.
elements.sent-2411-529: The task involves predicting the defect proneness of software modules based on various code metrics (e.g., complexity, coupling, cohesion).
elements.sent-2411-530: After selecting the representative tasks, we choose multiple baseline approaches for them.
elements.sent-2411-531: To select baselines for each task, we used the following criteria: (a) the technique should use a neural network model to perform one of the three tasks above, and (b) the technique should provide a comprehensive replication package with source code, datasets, and instructions for reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-532: We also look for baselines with clean and buggy versions of the data in their replication packages.
elements.sent-2411-533: The models take code tokens as input and learn to predict the vulnerability status of each line.
elements.sent-2411-534: The CNN model first learns to extract relevant features and patterns from a pair of bug reports and then predicts whether they are duplicates.
elements.sent-2411-535: BERT-MLP, a representation learning-based technique, uses the BERT model to learn the contextual relationship between words.
elements.sent-2411-536: Then, it uses a softmax-based classifier to determine if two bug reports are duplicates or not based on their representation.
elements.sent-2411-537: These techniques compute software metrics from datasets of code changes and extract salient features from commit messages.
elements.sent-2411-538: DeepJIT uses CNN to extract the salient features, whereas CodeBERTJIT utilizes CodeBERT to extract the features.
elements.sent-2411-539: Besides strong relevance to our selected data types, these baseline techniques have demonstrated state-of-the-art performance in their respective tasks, which justifies their selection for our study (Step \circled{3 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}

elements.sent-2411-540: The benchmark datasets utilized in our analysis exhibit several quality concerns that warrant discussion:.
elements.sent-2411-541: Vulnerability Detection (Devign, BigVul): Prior research has identified significant quality issues in these datasets~\cite{croft2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-542: Existing analysis indicates that only 54\% and 80\% of the samples in Big-Vul and Devign maintain correct labels, demonstrating substantial label noise~\cite{croft2023data .}
elements.sent-2411-543: Furthermore, these datasets face challenges with temporal relevance, as 15\% of Devign samples and 25\% of Big-Vul samples no longer reflect the current trends of vulnerability~\cite{croft2022data .}
elements.sent-2411-544: Duplicate Bug Report Detection (Hadoop, Eclipse): These datasets exhibit similar quality concerns regarding data representation and temporal validity.
elements.sent-2411-545: Studies have shown that only 5.2\% of Eclipse bug reports and 2.7\% of Hadoop bug reports are marked as duplicates~\cite{zhang2023duplicate .}
elements.sent-2411-546: Besides, the majority of their bug reports were submitted in 2014 or earlier, potentially leading to concept drift problems in these datasets~\cite{zhang2023duplicate .}
elements.sent-2411-547: Research has also demonstrated that approximately 25-40\% of bug reports in Bugzilla-based systems contain incorrect labels, indicating substantial label noise in these systems~\cite{tantithamthavorn2015impact .}
elements.sent-2411-548: Defect Prediction (OpenStack and QT): The OpenStack and QT datasets present multiple quality challenges.
elements.sent-2411-549: Research has revealed that their dataset construction methodology may introduce significant labelling errors, potentially affecting up to 55\% of the data~\cite{yatish2019icse .}
elements.sent-2411-550: These datasets also exhibit major class imbalance, with defective lines constituting only 20\% of the total samples~\cite{hoang2019deepjit .}
elements.sent-2411-551: Additionally, these datasets were constructed using commits before 2014, which might introduce significant concept drift in the techniques leveraging these datasets~\cite{hoang2019deepjit .}
elements.sent-2411-552: We also install the dependencies for each baseline into a separate virtual environment using the venv\footnote{https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html module.}
elements.sent-2411-553: We removed the preprocessing operations from our training pipeline dealing with different types of data (Step \circled{6 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ): for code-based data, we omitted the stop word removal, line separation, and sequence length normalization; for text-based data, we excluded stemming, stop word removal, and case conversion; and for metric-based data, we eliminated data normalization and feature scaling.}}
elements.sent-2411-554: W\&B's built-in logging capabilities and predefined hooks facilitated the extraction of gradients, weights, and biases.
elements.sent-2411-555: After configuring W\&B to watch the model parameters, it automatically tracked these metrics and attached hooks to each layer, eliminating the need for manual implementation.
elements.sent-2411-556: During training, W\&B captured the full distribution of gradients flowing through each layer, weight matrices, and bias vectors.
elements.sent-2411-557: The hooks tracked gradient changes during backpropagation, providing insights into the learning dynamics of different layers.
elements.sent-2411-558: These metrics were stored in W\&B's dashboard for detailed analysis of the model's internal dynamics throughout the training process.
elements.sent-2411-559: We implemented this integration across all model variants to maintain consistency in our experimental methodology.
elements.sent-2411-560: Additionally, we logged runtime configurations to document the computational environment.
elements.sent-2411-561: To account for the variability and stochasticity inherent in neural networks, we ran each baseline model and corresponding variants fifteen times\cite{arcuri2014hitchhiker .}
elements.sent-2411-562: We developed a data-driven approach to identify symptoms of data quality and preprocessing issues in deep learning models.
elements.sent-2411-563: Our methodology consists of four key steps: First, we established baseline parameter distributions by training 15 models on clean, properly preprocessed data.
elements.sent-2411-564: For each model layer, we collected descriptive statistics of weights, biases, and gradients during training.
elements.sent-2411-565: In particular, we capture the mean, median, maximum, minimum, variance, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis and sparsity of these measures.
elements.sent-2411-566: These statistics characterize the expected behaviors of properly trained models and serve as our reference point.
elements.sent-2411-567: Second, we trained multiple models on datasets containing known preprocessing and data quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-568: Similar to the first step, we captured descriptive statistics of weights, biases and gradients.
elements.sent-2411-569: Third, we aggregated these statistics layer-by-layer across multiple models to identify common patterns.
elements.sent-2411-570: We then compared the aggregated statistics between the bug-free and buggy models to detect deviations in the buggy model's statistics, using the bug-free model as our reference.
elements.sent-2411-571: We also flagged outliers in the buggy model's statistics using three complementary statistical methods:.
elements.sent-2411-572: Z-Score analysis for weights and biases: Computing mean ($\mu$) and standard deviation ($\sigma$) identify data points with high Z-score, $|Z|>3$ (i.e., $|X-\mu|>3\sigma$)~\cite{tukey1977exploratory .}
elements.sent-2411-573: Interquartile Range (IQR) for gradients: Calculating 25th ($Q_1$) and 75th ($Q_3$) percentiles to identify values outside $[Q_1-1.5\times IQR, Q_3+1.5\times IQR]$~\cite{leys2013detecting .}
elements.sent-2411-574: Distribution shape analysis: Measuring skewness ($>1$) and kurtosis ($|kurtosis|>3$) to detect distribution distortions~\cite{westfall2014kurtosis .}
elements.sent-2411-575: By capturing the above statistics from model parameters, we attempted to detect consistent statistical patterns that could serve as reliable indicators of data bugs in the DL models.
elements.sent-2411-576: Finally, we analyzed statistics and averaged them across models and layers where these patterns appeared consistently in the buggy models.
elements.sent-2411-577: We then compared these against our bug-free models, which provided essential reference distributions of normal parameter behavior.
elements.sent-2411-578: These bug-free models were crucial for establishing reliable statistical thresholds and understanding what constitutes expected parameter distributions in properly trained models.
elements.sent-2411-579: By contrasting the buggy models' statistics against these reference distributions, we could precisely identify which deviations represented genuine data quality issues rather than acceptable variations.
elements.sent-2411-580: These thresholds were also validated against validation datasets to confirm their effectiveness in detecting known data quality issues.
elements.sent-2411-581: This process resulted in a set of reliable statistical criteria that can help identify symptoms and their prevalence in model training.
elements.subsection-2411-017: Determining the Impact on Downstream Performance
elements.sent-2411-582: After determining the symptoms of data quality and preprocessing issues, we determine the impact of these symptoms on the performance of the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-583: To determine how individual symptoms affect model performance while controlling for confounding factors; we performed a systematic analysis of models exhibiting isolated symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-584: For each symptom identified through our quantitative analysis, we filtered our experimental runs to obtain a subset of models that displayed only that particular symptom, excluding any models that exhibited multiple concurrent symptoms.
elements.sent-2411-585: This filtering process ensured that our analysis focused solely on the direct relationship between individual symptoms and performance degradation.
elements.sent-2411-586: We then aggregated performance metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) across all models that uniquely exhibited each type of symptom.
elements.sent-2411-587: By analyzing models with isolated symptoms, we could attribute performance changes to specific symptoms without the confounding effects of symptom co-occurrence.
elements.sent-2411-588: To gain deeper insights into the impacts of data bugs on our deep learning models, we conducted a post-hoc analysis using Explainable AI techniques (Step \circled{8 , Fig.~\ref{fig:schematicDiagram ).}}
elements.sent-2411-589: These techniques help us identify the influential parts of the input data and understand how the models make predictions.
elements.sent-2411-590: Given an attention weight tensor $A^l \in \mathbb{R ^{h \times n \times n}$ from layer $l$, which contains attention weights for $h$ heads and $n$ tokens, we first average the attention weights across all heads.}
elements.sent-2411-591: For each token pair $(j,k)$ in the attention matrix, we compute the mean attention weight $\bar{A ^l_{jk}$ using: \begin{equation}\bar{A}^l_{jk} = \frac{1}{h}\sum_{i=1}^{h}A^l_{i,j,k}\end{equation} This gives us a single $n \times n$ attention matrix (for n tokens) that represents the average attention weights across all heads.}
elements.sent-2411-592: To quantify the overall importance of each token $j$ in the sequence, we sum up all the attention weights directed towards that token using: \begin{equation}score_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\bar{A}^l_{ij}\end{equation} This produces a scalar $score_j$ for each token $j$, representing how much attention that token receives from all other tokens on average.
elements.sent-2411-593: When calculated for all tokens, this results in a vector of importance scores $[score_1, ..., score_n]$, which measures each token's relative importance in the context.
elements.sent-2411-594: By applying t-SNE to the representations learned by the DCCNN model~\cite{he2020duplicate at different layers, we visualized the decision boundary for duplicate and non-duplicate bug reports.}
elements.sent-2411-595: Given high-dimensional embeddings $X = {x_1, ..., x_N}$ and their low-dimensional counterparts $Y = {y_1, ..., y_N}$, t-SNE minimizes their distribution gap by employing the Kullback-Leibler divergence: \begin{equation}KL(P||Q) = \sum_{i}\sum_{j}p_{ij}\log\frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}\end{equation} where $p_{ij}$ and $q_{ij}$ are the pairwise similarities of the duplicate bug reports in the high and low-dimensional spaces, respectively.
